summary: Yegge writes a casual status update after a minor scare where an automated system disabled his Google account, leading Reddit to speculate he was being fired. He announces he's working on a book about Mozilla Rhino with Norris Boyd, building a JavaScript interpreter in Emacs-Lisp to create a world-class JavaScript IDE and allow writing Emacs extensions in JavaScript. He also weighs in on the ECMAScript 4 debate, obliquely criticizing Microsoft's opposition, and reaffirms his love for working at Google.
key_points: An automated system accidentally disabled his Google account, causing brief panic and Reddit speculation about his firing
He's co-writing a book on Mozilla Rhino with Norris Boyd, the primary author of Rhino
He's building a full JavaScript interpreter in Emacs-Lisp to create a JavaScript IDE for Emacs and allow writing Emacs extensions in JS instead of elisp
He criticizes 'Uncle Mike' (Microsoft) for opposing ECMAScript 4 and encourages people to switch to Firefox
He's experimenting with limiting blog posts to ~1000 words to publish more frequently
money_quotes: I had an exciting morning of not getting fired today.
This automated system had apparently just watched the movie Brazil, and thought it would be fun to send me off to Information Retrieval.
I wish I could get back to High School Time, where even a single hour-long class can seem like eternity, and 4 years is effectively infinite.
JavaScript turns out to be a better language, now that I know them both in excruciating detail.
Emacs is a great environment that needs a better extension language.
That's the elevator pitch: puts people to sleep in 20 seconds, guaranteed.
I still use Emacs to an extent that could justifiably be described as 'unhealthy'.
Like my friend Dominic says, 'it feels like I've won the lottery every day.'
key_insight: JavaScript is poised to become a Next Big Language, and its elegance makes it a better choice than Emacs-Lisp for extending Emacs despite the unconventional implementation path.
